title: Rogue Process Hunt
scenario: Three rogue 'sleep 1000' processes were accidentally left running on a production server, consuming process-table slots and making it harder to audit what is actually running. A senior engineer has asked you to locate all of them, record their PIDs for the incident report, and terminate them cleanly.
symptoms: Unexpected 'sleep 1000' processes appear in the process list
Running 'ps aux | grep sleep' reveals multiple zombie-like sleep processes
Process table is cluttered with processes that should not be running
mission: Use 'ps aux' and 'grep' (or 'pgrep') to locate the three rogue sleep processes. Write all three PIDs to /home/intern/pids_found.txt (one PID per line). Then kill one of them by PID using 'kill', and terminate the remaining sleep processes with 'pkill sleep'.

hints.title: List all running processes
hints.content: Run 'ps aux' to see every process on the system. The output shows USER, PID, %CPU, %MEM, and the COMMAND. Look for lines where the COMMAND column says 'sleep 1000'.

hints.title: Filter with grep or pgrep
hints.content: Pipe ps output through grep: 'ps aux | grep sleep'. Alternatively, 'pgrep sleep' prints just the PIDs of matching processes — one per line, ready to copy.

hints.title: Record the PIDs
hints.content: Write the PIDs to /home/intern/pids_found.txt, one per line. You can do this manually with a text editor, or use a redirect: 'pgrep sleep > /home/intern/pids_found.txt'.

hints.title: Kill processes
hints.content: Use 'kill <PID>' to terminate a single process by its PID. Then run 'pkill sleep' to kill all remaining processes whose name matches 'sleep'. Verify with 'pgrep sleep' — it should return nothing.

acceptance_criteria.description: pids_found.txt exists and lists at least 3 sleep process PIDs
all rogue sleep processes have been killed (none remain)
